explanation: Complete Blood Count — measures red/white blood cells, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelets.
Magnetic Resonance Imaging — uses magnetic fields and radio waves for detailed organ imaging. No radiation.
Computed Tomography — uses X-rays from multiple angles for cross-sectional images. Faster than MRI.
Electrocardiogram (EKG) — records heart electrical activity. Detects arrhythmias and cardiac conditions.
Electroencephalogram — measures brain electrical activity. Diagnoses epilepsy and brain disorders.
Blood Pressure — force on arterial walls. Normal: <120/80 mmHg. Hypertension: >=130/80.
Body Mass Index — weight/height ratio (kg/m2). Normal: 18.5-24.9, Overweight: 25-29.9, Obese: >=30.
Intensive Care Unit — critical care department for seriously ill patients requiring close monitoring.
Intravenous — delivery of fluids/medications directly into a vein.
Intramuscular — injection into muscle. Sites: deltoid, gluteus, vastus lateralis.
Pro Re Nata (as needed) — take medication only when required.
Bis In Die — twice daily.
Ter In Die — three times daily.
Immediately (Latin: statim) — give treatment right away.
Per Os — by mouth (oral administration).
Nil Per Os — nothing by mouth, typically before surgery.
Do Not Resuscitate — no CPR if heart/breathing stops.
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease — progressive lung diseases blocking airflow.
Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease — chronic acid reflux into the esophagus.
Urinary Tract Infection — infection in kidneys, ureters, bladder, or urethra.
Deep Vein Thrombosis — blood clot in deep vein (usually leg). Risk of PE.
Pulmonary Embolism — blocked pulmonary artery, usually from DVT. Emergency.
Myocardial Infarction (Heart Attack) — heart muscle death from blocked blood supply.
Cerebrovascular Accident (Stroke) — interrupted brain blood supply. Ischemic or hemorrhagic.
Transient Ischemic Attack (Mini-stroke) — temporary brain blood flow blockage. Stroke warning.
Congestive Heart Failure — heart can't pump efficiently. Fluid buildup in lungs/tissues.
International Normalized Ratio — clotting time test. Monitors warfarin. Target: 2.0-3.0.
Glycated Hemoglobin — 2-3 month blood sugar average. Normal <5.7%, Diabetes >=6.5%.
Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate — kidney function. Normal >90. CKD if <60.
C-Reactive Protein — inflammation marker. Elevated in infection and autoimmune disease.
White Blood Cell count — elevated in infection, decreased in immunosuppression.
High Blood Pressure — persistent >=130/80 mmHg. Risk factor for heart disease and stroke.
Low Blood Pressure — below 90/60 mmHg. Causes dizziness, fainting, shock.
Fast heart rate — >100 bpm at rest. Causes: fever, anxiety, anemia, heart disease.
Slow heart rate — <60 bpm. Normal in athletes; pathological in heart block.
Irregular heartbeat — includes atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, heart block.
Difficulty breathing / shortness of breath. Causes: heart failure, pneumonia, asthma.
Swelling from excess tissue fluid. Causes: heart failure, kidney disease, venous insufficiency.
Low red blood cells / hemoglobin. Symptoms: fatigue, weakness, pallor.
Life-threatening infection response. Can cause organ failure and death.
Insufficient blood supply causing oxygen deprivation. Affects heart, brain, limbs.
Blood clot inside a vessel obstructing flow. Venous or arterial.
Abnormal narrowing of a channel. Examples: aortic stenosis, spinal stenosis.
Chronic liver scarring. Causes: alcohol, hepatitis, fatty liver.
Cancer spread from primary tumor to distant organs via blood/lymphatics.
Tissue sample removal for microscopic examination. Diagnoses cancer and other conditions.
Camera tube to examine body interior. Types: gastroscopy, colonoscopy, bronchoscopy.
Blood filtering when kidneys fail. Types: hemodialysis, peritoneal.
Tube insertion into trachea for airway maintenance or ventilation.
Condition making a treatment inadvisable due to potential harm.
Time for drug blood concentration to halve. Determines dosing frequency.
Drug proportion reaching systemic circulation. IV = 100%.
How body processes drugs: absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME).
How drugs affect the body: mechanism, therapeutic effects, side effects.
